title: Lavender infusion for mild low mood
subtitle: A gentle nervine infusion for moments of low spirits
excerpt: A hot infusion of dried lavender flowers, taken for mild, situational low mood and emotional flatness. Lavender's nervine and mood-lifting properties are among the best-studied herbal actions. This is a supportive preparation for mild, temporary low mood, not a treatment for depression.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), lavender entry, nervine, antidepressant, and restorative properties. EMA HMPC monograph on Lavandulae flos (2011), confirms traditional use for anxiety, restlessness, and sleep disturbance. Silexan (standardised lavender oil preparation) clinical trials, Kasper et al. (2014, 2015), anxiolytic and mood effects in clinical trials, though at higher concentration than a kitchen infusion.

content.text: A cup of lavender tea for a difficult day. Lavender is the herbal tradition's most studied

for emotional wellbeing. Its main active, linalool, acts on the brain's calming pathways and supports mood. A standardised lavender oil pill, Silexan, has been tested in human trials. At a much higher strength than a tea, it eases anxiety and steadies mood. The kitchen cup is a gentler, quieter form. It suits mild, situational low mood, not clinical depression. The kind of emotional flatness that comes with a hard stretch, built-up stress, or a seasonal dip. It is also simply a pleasant, fragrant cup of tea.

attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a medical professional for ongoing or serious symptoms.

This cup is for mild, short-term low mood. The kind that lifts with rest, talking, and small pleasures. It is not a treatment for clinical depression. Clinical depression is a serious medical condition that needs proper assessment and care.

Seek medical care if low mood goes on for a long stretch, or if it brings changes in sleep, appetite, or focus. Seek care if it is hard to work or to hold relationships. Seek care if you have lost interest in things you used to enjoy, or if you have any thoughts of hopelessness, self-harm, or suicide. These are signs of depression and need medical attention.

Pregnancy: lavender infusion at normal tea strength is widely used in pregnancy and has no known harmful effects.

St John's Wort, not this preparation, is the herbal medicine with the strongest record for mild to moderate depression. It interacts with many medicines, so see a herbalist before using it.
attrs.title: Important safety notes

items.name: Dried lavender flowers
items.prepNote: about 1 teaspoon of dried flowers, lavender is fragrant and a little goes a long way

items.name: Honey
items.prepNote: optional, lavender tea can be slightly bitter; honey softens this

content.text: Place {{dried-lavender-flowers}} of dried lavender flowers in the teapot or infuser. Pour {{water}} of just-off-boil water over and lid immediately. Steep for exactly 10 minutes, beyond that, the floral note tips towards a soap-like quality. Strain and drink slowly. The scent is at least as therapeutic as the cup itself; sit with the cup for a moment before drinking.

One cup once or twice daily during a period of low mood or emotional stress. Lavender at normal infusion strength is safe for daily long-term use. There are no significant safety concerns at one to two cups per day.
When NOT to use this remedy

Seek medical care if low mood goes on for a long stretch, or if it brings changes in sleep, appetite, or focus. Seek care if it is affecting your ability to work or hold relationships, or if you have any thoughts of self-harm or hopelessness. The lavender cup is for mild, short-term low mood. Depression needs proper medical care.
